examples.title: Process Creation with fork() and exec()
examples.description: Demonstrates how to create a child process and execute a new program
examples.explanation: fork() creates a child process that is a copy of parent. In child process (pid==0), execl() replaces the program with /bin/ls. Parent uses waitpid() to wait for child termination.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is program execution time

examples.title: File Operations using System Calls
examples.description: Demonstrates file manipulation through system calls in Python
examples.code:
import os

# Open a file (system call: open)
fd = os.open("test.txt", os.O_CREAT | os.O_WRONLY, 0o644)

# Write to file (system call: write)
data = b"Hello, System Calls!"
os.write(fd, data)

# Close file (system call: close)
os.close(fd)

# Reopen and read (system calls: open, read, close)
fd = os.open("test.txt", os.O_RDONLY)
content = os.read(fd, 100)
os.close(fd)

print("Read content:", content.decode())
examples.explanation: Python's os module provides direct wrappers around system calls. os.open() invokes the open() syscall, os.write() invokes write(), os.read() invokes read(), and os.close() invokes close().

examples.title: Inter-Process Communication with pipe()
examples.description: Demonstrates creating a pipe for parent-child communication
examples.explanation: pipe() creates a unidirectional channel with two file descriptors. pipefd[0] is for reading, pipefd[1] is for writing. Parent writes to pipefd[1], child reads from pipefd[0].
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is message size

examples.title: General System Call Execution Mechanism
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how system calls are invoked and executed
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the typical flow: user code places syscall number and parameters in registers, triggers a trap/interrupt, kernel validates and executes the service, then returns control with result.
